coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescent probe based on PCN-224 for rapid and ultrasensitive detection of copper ions
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Wenjie
Hu, Zhe
Wei, Jinxin
Lu, Hanxu
Dai, Hanqing
Zhao, Jinchan
Zhang, Wanlu
Guo, Ruiqian
coredata.dc:description: Copper is a ubiquitous metal in the environment. Due to the complexity of traditional detection techniques, searching for a selective and sensitive probe to quantify the concentration of copper ions is of great significance. Herein, a novel fluorescent probe (CDs-PCN) was synthesized by the post-synthetic modification of PCN-224 with carbon dots. In this probe, the emission of carbon dots was utilized as a reference signal, and the emission of PCN-224 was taken as a sensitive response signal with the active sites for detecting copper ions. Due to the rapid, sensitive and selective response to copper ions, CDs-PCN is exploited as an excellent ratiometric fluorescence probe for detecting copper ions with a low detection limit of 44 nM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Metal-organic frameworks
Fluorescent sensing
Ratiometric

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM micrographs, size distribution histograms and TEM micrographs of PCN-224(a) (c) (e) and CDs-PCN(b) (d) (f); (g) HRTEM micrographs of CDs-PCN; (h) The PXRD patterns of PCN-224, CDs-PCN and simulated PCN-224.
(a) The FT-IR spectra of PCN-224, CDs-PCN and CDs, a partial magnification is shown on the right; XPS spectra and Zr 3d spectra of CDs-PCN (b) (c) and PCN-224 (d) (e).
(a) The PL spectra and (b) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs, PCN-224 and CDs-PCN.

(a) PL spectra of CDs-PCN with various Cu2+ concentrations from 0 to 10 μM (λex = 400 nm); (b) The linear variation of I466/I655 against Cu2+ concentrations; (c) PL spectra of blank probe dispersion at different measurements; (d) Fluorescence response of probe to various coexisting ions.
Process for the solvothermal synthesis of PCN-224 and the PSM of PCN-224 with CDs.
Previous LMOFs-based fluorescence probes for Cu2+.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescent probe based on PCN-224 for rapid and ultrasensitive detection of copper ions

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Information Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Copper is a ubiquitous metal in the environment. Due to the complexity of traditional detection techniques, searching for a selective and sensitive probe to quantify the concentration of copper ions is of great significance. Herein, a novel fluorescent probe (CDs-PCN) was synthesized by the post-synthetic modification of PCN-224 with carbon dots. In this probe, the emission of carbon dots was utilized as a reference signal, and the emission of PCN-224 was taken as a sensitive response signal with the active sites for detecting copper ions. Due to the rapid, sensitive and selective response to copper ions, CDs-PCN is exploited as an excellent ratiometric fluorescence probe for detecting copper ions with a low detection limit of 44 nM.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper is a ubiquitous metal in the environment. As a vital role in cellular processes, the concentration of copper ions (Cu2+) has a significant impact on human health. Copper deficiency can lead to anemia or bone abnormalities, while copper excess can result in vomiting, diarrhea and even kidney damage [1]. In addition, excessive copper leakage through various pathways brings about pollution to the ecosystem. Particularly, the limits of copper concentration stipulated by the World Health Organization (WHO) and the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) are 2 ppm (∼30 μM) and 1.3 ppm (∼20 μM), respectively. In the past few years, several detection techniques have been applied for Cu2+ like inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES), electrochemical methods, and so on [2–4]. However, these methods may suffer from expensive equipment, time-consuming sample preparation and complicated procedures, which limit their application. In contrast, fluorescent sensing is a suitable method for the detection of Cu2+, with merits of simplicity, rapidity, high sensitivity and selectivity [5].

In the past two decades, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have attracted extensive attention of researchers. They are porous materials formed by metal ions and organic ligands. Among the huge number of MOFs, luminescent MOFs (LMOFs) are endowed with determinable structure, high porosity and adjustable fluorescence [6–8]. Due to their key advantages for use as fluorescent sensors, LMOFs have great potential for applications in detecting various analytes like explosives [9,10], ions [11–13], toxic species [14,15], biomolecules [16,17], volatile organic compounds [18,19], etc.

Most fluorescent probes are based on the turn-on or turn-off mechanism of a single signal [20,21]. For example, Li et al. [22] presented a porphyrinic MOF as a turn-off chemosensor for Cu2+. The accessible recognition sites provided by porphyrin moieties endowed the probe with an outstanding detection limit performance. However, the sensing results based on a single signal can easily be affected by the background fluorescence and environmental interference. Thus, ratiometric fluorescence probes are considered as suitable tools to fabricate sensing platforms by introducing dual or multiple luminescent signals [23,24]. Taking the ratio of different signals, ratiometric fluorescence probes can greatly minimize the interferences from external conditions and improve the sensing accuracy, sensitivity and selectivity due to the self-calibrating mechanism. As a new category of semiconductor materials, quantum dots (QDs) are of excellent fluorescence properties and high stability compared with traditional fluorescent materials like organic dyes, which can be compounded in MOFs to achieve stable ratiometric fluorescence probes [25,26]. Among.

Various categories of QDs, carbon dots (CDs) possess advantages of low toxicity, facile synthesis, good biocompatibility, high aqueous solubility and photochemical stability, demonstrating the potential for biomedical applications [27,28].

Herein, a ratiometric probe (CDs-PCN) is designed for Cu2+ detection based on LMOFs and QDs. The ratiometric outputs of CDs-PCN show a good linear correlation versus the concentration of Cu2+ at a large slope with a limit of detection (LOD) at 44 nM, indicating that CDs-PCN is capable of monitoring the Cu2+ levels in aqueous solutions accurately and sensitively.
The details about the preparation and characterization of the probe were provided in the Supporting Information.

As shown in Scheme 1 , CDs-PCN is prepared by post-synthetic modification (PSM) of PCN-224 with CDs through combining the abundant groups on the surface of CDs with the unsaturated sites provided by the activated PCN-224. After PSM with CDs, the probe went through a color variation from dark brown to dark purple, suggesting the incorporation of CDs into PCN-224 (Fig. S1). SEM and TEM micrographs were obtained to characterize the morphology and size of CDs-PCN. As shown in Fig. 1 , both CDs-PCN and PCN-224 are of a good monodispersity and uniform bulk morphology. With a larger average size of 698 nm, CDs-PCN also possesses a rougher surface due to the PSM procedure with CDs. The CDs in the probe can be clearly characterized in the high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) micrographs (Fig. 1g) with good crystallization and distinct lattice fringe, indicating the successful incorporation of CDs. The powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns were measured to prove the successful preparation of PCN-224 in Fig. 1h. Both CDs-PCN and PCN-224 show main diffraction peaks at 6.4°, 7.9°, 9.1°, 11.2°, and 13.7°, which are in agreement with the simulated PXRD of PCN-224, indicating that the introduction of CDs has almost no effects on the original crystalline structure of PCN-224 [29].
The N2 adsorption measurement was carried out to confirm the porosity of the probe. As shown in Fig. S2, both PCN-224 and CDs-PCN.

Exhibit type I isotherms. The adsorption quantity of CDs-PCN is much higher than that of PCN-224, and the BET surface areas are 453.096 and 1990.741 m2 g−1 respectively for PCN-224 and CDs-PCN. The unique properties of QDs endow them with a large specific surface area. As the average size of the prepared CDs is larger than the pore diameter of PCN-224 (Fig. S3), only part of the CDs can occupy the pore and most of the CDs were incorporated on the surface of PCN-224 through the coordination, resulting in the great increase in the BET surface area and a slight decrease in pore diameter [30]. According to the textural data obtained through BJH measurements in Table S1, the total pore volume of CDs-PCN rises from 0.061 cm3 g−1 to 0.107 cm3 g−1, implying that the introduction of CDs leads to the formation of new pores at the bound interface due to the uneven distribution of CDs on the surface of PCN-224 [31], which can be proved by the rougher surface after PSM (Fig. 1f). Such a high surface area and large pore window make it more feasible for the probe to identify the analytes through recognition sites [32]. Fig. S4 shows the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) curves of PCN-224 and CDs-PCN, and the loading amounts of incorporated CDs was confirmed at 13.947% by the difference in residual mass before and after the PSM procedure with CDs.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was employed to further study the chemical composition. Compared with pristine PCN-224, the peaks of CDs-PCN at 3450 cm−1, 2925 cm−1 and 1145 cm−1 remain unchanged, which were respectively assigned to the stretching vibration of O–H, C–H and C–O bonds. The characteristic stretching vibration of CO bond of the free carboxylic groups at 1655 cm−1 becomes greatly

Weakened, and a new FT-IR peak appears at 818 cm−1. A similar peak at 818 cm−1 can also be found in the spectrum of CDs, which is attributed to the bending vibration of C–H bond in CDs, suggesting presence of CDs in the probe [33,34]. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were measured to characterize the composition on the surface. The survey spectra (Fig. 2 b) apparently demonstrate the existence of Zr, C, O and N in CDs-PCN. As shown in Fig. 2c and e, the high-resolution Zr 3d XPS spectra of PCN-224 reveal that the Zr 3d3/2 and Zr 3d5/2 peaks of PCN-224 are located at 185.0 eV and 182.6 eV respectively, while both of the peaks shifted to a lower binding energy by 0.1 eV in the spectrum of CDs-PCN. Moreover, we calculated the area ratios of the Zr 3d5/2 and Zr 3d3/2 peaks in both PCN-224 and CDs-PCN and found that the proportion of Zr 3d3/2 decreased slightly in the probe. These changes indicate the redistribution of the charge within Zr6 nodes after PSM [34,35], implying the successful coordination of the abundant active groups of CDs to the free carboxylic groups of the Zr6 nodes of the activated PCN-224.

Subsequently, we studied the spectroscopic properties of the probe. A typical peak at 466 nm appears in the PL spectrum of CDs-PCN (Fig. 3 a) in contrast to that of PCN-224, which can be attributed to the emission of the incorporated CDs. With the excitation wavelength at 400 nm, the fluorescence spectrum of CDs-PCN shows a strong emission at 655 nm and a weak emission shoulder at 715 nm, corresponding to the typical transition of porphyrin from the first excited singlet state S1 to the ground state S0 [36]. The composite materials possess both the characteristic peaks simultaneously, implying the materials contain PCN-224 and CDs. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs-PCN in Fig. 3b demonstrates a Soret band of porphyrin at 450 nm and four characteristic absorption Q-bands at 527, 558, 593 and 643 nm of TCPP, which are quite similar to the bands of PCN-224, indicating that the porphyrin units in CDs-PCN are well-isolated in the materials and exist in the form of free TCPP [37]. The fluorescence of the probe remains stable in pH = 3–11 aqueous solutions according to the stability tests (Fig. S5).

With purpose of exploring the sensing property of CDs-PCN probe towards Cu2+, the as-synthesized probe was applied to detect various Cu2+ concentrations in HEPES buffer. The response time was evaluated in Fig. S6. In presence of Cu2+, the I655/I466 ratios sharply decrease within 30 s and remain stable after 60 s, revealing the rapid response and detection ability of CDs-PCN. Fig. 4 a shows the concentration-dependent fluorescence response curve of the probe at 25 °C. With the excitation wavelength at 400 nm, the emission peak of CDs at 466 nm is utilized as a reference signal, while the emission peak at 655 nm is applied as the detection signal. With the gradual increase of the Cu2+ concentration ranging from 0 to 10 μM, the PL intensity of CDs-PCN at 655 nm gradually decreases but remains almost unchanged at 466 nm. In addition, the PL intensity ratios I466/I655 increase continuously responding.

To the constant increase of Cu2+ concentrations with a good linearity ranging from 0 to 10 μM, and the linear correlation is I466/I655 = 2.867C + 0.149, R2 = 0.995 (Fig. 4b). The standard deviation σ is determined by the PL intensity of the blank probe dispersion at different measurements (Fig. 4c). Taking the signal-to-noise (S/N) ratio as 3, the LOD is calculated to be 44 nM, which is much lower than the standard concentrations set by WHO and EPA. Moreover, the fluorescence of the CDs-PCN dispersion turns from red to blue (Fig. S7a). The coordinates of fluorescence color under various Cu2+ concentrations are marked in the CIE chromaticity diagram in Fig. S7b, which fit well with a linear relationship as y = 0.921x+0.1, R2 = 0.968, implying that CDs-PCN shows the potential to be a colorimetric fluorescence probe for Cu2+ detection. Some previous LMOFs-based fluorescence probes towards Cu2+ are listed in Table 1 , and CDs-PCN is quite competitive with a LOD lower than most of previously reported probes.

Besides sensitivity, selectivity is also a vital factor to evaluate the probe. To figure out the selectivity, the PL intensity of probe solution was recorded in presence of some possible coexisting cations (Zn2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Li+, K+, Na+, Ag+, Fe3+, Fe2+ and Ba2+) and anions (SO4 2−, SO3 2−, NO3 −, Cl−, F−, CH3COO− and CO3 2−). As shown in Fig. 4d, the fluorescence ratio I466/I655 can only decrease sharply in presence of Cu2+, while the influences of other ions can almost be ignored. The results determine that the CDs-PCN possesses excellent selectivity for Cu2+. To further explore the mechanism, the PXRD spectra variation of CDs-PCN after introduction of Cu2+ is given in Fig. S8a. The PXRD spectrum after adding Cu2+ is quite different from the original one. Almost all the characteristic peaks of PCN-224 disappeared. The UV–Vis absorption spectra were further measured in Fig. S8b. The typical Q-band at 527 nm was red-shifted and the other three Q-bands almost vanished. Therefore, we speculated a mechanism for the specific Cu2+ detection of the probe. Due to the affinity of TCPP for Cu2+, Cu2+ tends to bind with the active sites of TCPP [43], leading to the collapse of crystalline structure of CDs-PCN, which affects the fluorescence properties of the probe [41,44].

In short, we synthesized a novel ratiometric fluorescent CD-PCN probe through the PSM of PCN-224 with CDs. The probe was proposed for Cu2+ detection in a wide linear range with a very low LOD of 44 nM. To our knowledge, it is the first time that Cu2+ have been detected utilizing a ratiometric fluorescence probe based on a composite of LMOFs and QDs. Taking the fluorescence of CDs as a reference signal and PCN-224 as a sensitive response signal, CDs-PCN possesses excellent accuracy, high sensitivity and good selectivity, showing potential for being an effective Cu2+ probe in more complex environments.

Wenjie Zhou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Zhe Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Jinxin Wei: Software, Formal analysis. Hanxu Lu: Software, Formal analysis. Hanqing Dai: Writing – review & editing. Jinchan Zhao: Formal analysis. Wanlu Zhang: Supervision. Ruiqian Guo: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 62074044 and NSFC, No. 61904036), Zhongshan - Fudan Joint Innovation Center and Jihua Laboratory Projects of Guangdong Province (X190111UZ190).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Neodymium(III) organic frameworks (Nd-MOF) as near infrared fluorescent probe for highly selectively sensing of Cu2+

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Luo, B.S. Liu, C. Cao, F. Wei, Neodymium(III) organic frameworks (Nd-MOF) as near infrared fluorescent probe for highly selectively sensing of Cu2+, Inorg. Chem. Commun. 76 (2017) 18-21.

Chem. 59(9) (2020) 6202-6213.
